policy_statement: Require zero onsite fossil fuel emissions from all new buildings by 2021; require zero onsite fossil fuel emissions from all large existing commercial buildings by 2035; and achieve zero emissions for all buildings by 2040.
mechanism_description: The city is requiring new buildings and large existing commercial buildings (and ultimately all buildings) to have zero onsite fossil fuel emissions, eliminating onsite combustion emissions from building energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy end-use change (electrification) → ↓ onsite fossil fuel combustion → ↓ direct (Scope 1) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Building energy systems shift from fossil-fueled combustion appliances/equipment (e.g., gas boilers, furnaces, water heaters, cooking) to electric or other zero-onsite-emissions alternatives (e.g., heat pumps, electric resistance where applicable, district energy with no onsite combustion).  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced oxidation/combustion of fossil fuels onsite eliminates CO₂ emissions and also reduces combustion-related methane slip and nitrous oxide where relevant.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct operational GHG emissions from the building stock; additional indirect benefits can accrue over time as grid electricity decarbonizes (though the defining mechanism is the onsite combustion elimination).
dominant_pathway_test: Eliminating onsite fossil fuel combustion in buildings most directly reduces greenhouse gas emissions from building operations (and associated climate forcing).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Electrification/zero onsite fossil fuel requirement → eliminates onsite combustion → reduces direct GHG emissions from buildings.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve air quality, safety, or resilience, the single biggest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions mitigation via removing fossil combustion.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is carbon-intensive, near-term emissions could shift upstream; however, the mechanism still targets GHG mitigation by removing combustion at point of use and enabling decarbonization as grids clean up.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that requires “zero onsite fossil fuel emissions” or bans onsite combustion shares the same primary mitigation pathway (direct emissions elimination from building operations).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer labeling (e.g., “building electrification,” “fuel switching,” “zero-emission buildings,” “retrofit vs new-build”) depends on policy-specific scope, compliance metrics, and grid context and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential emissions shifting to the power sector if the grid is fossil-heavy; possible inclusion of district energy or renewable fuels under “zero onsite emissions” definitions; backup generators and process loads may be treated differently in some codes—these affect implementation details but not the primary mitigation classification.
key_indicators: “requiring zero onsite fossil fuel emissions”; “Zero emissions new construction by 2021”; “All large commercial buildings are zero emissions by 2035”; “All buildings zero emissions by 2040”; “CLIMATE ACTION GOALS”
co_benefits: Improved indoor/outdoor air quality and public health from reduced combustion pollutants; potential energy cost savings; improved building safety (reduced combustion/fire/CO risks).
instance_edge_case_notes: The excerpt lists “goals” but uses the word “requiring,” implying enforceable standards/mandates; without more context on legal form, it is treated as a performance standard rather than a pure target-setting statement.
